title: 2026/03/16 ENV (Part 2)
description: Monday, 16 March 2026 | Environment Committee Planning Bill; Natural Environment Bill Dairy NZ David Cooper, Principal Policy Advisor Dugald MacTavish Linda Conning Jocelyn Papprill Suzanne Hills Dianne Downward Eugenie Sage Andy Mitchell
descriptionSummary: Planning Bill. Planning Bill; Natural Environment Bill. Dairy NZ

chunks.text: Dairy NZ.

And we can see you there, David. Um, we just-- oh, there we go. Welcome to the select committee this morning. You have twenty minutes with us. Um, do take your submission as read. I know it's a, a pretty weighty submission here, but, we'll hand it over to you to talk us through the main points, and then we'll open up for questions.

Kia ora koutou. Uh, just making sure you can see Richard McIntyre there as well, our board member. Uh, no, we can't see Richard at this point. So- He's in it. Um, if we-- oh, there we go. We can just see him pop through now. Welcome, Richard. Hey, thank you very much for having us.

Um, so tena koutou katoa, chair and members of the Environment Select Committee. I'm Richard McIntyre, and I'm a dairy farmer and farmer-elected director of Dairy NZ. With me today is David Cooper, who you've just talked to before. He's our principal policy advisor. Dairy NZ is the industry good organization representing all New Zealand, all New Zealand dairy farmers.

We are funded by a levy on milk solids and exist to help farmers build profitable, sustainable, resilient businesses through extension, advocacy, science and research. Dairy is one of New Zealand's largest export earners, with twenty-seven billion dollars in export earnings in the year to June 2025, representing roughly one in every four export dollars.

The sector directly employs around twenty-four thousand people, increasing to an estimated fifty-five thousand people if you include processing and farm support services. As a farmer, I've seen firsthand how the sector has stepped up. Over the last decade, dairy farmers have widely adopted practices like waterway fencing, improved effluent management, nutrient budgeting and farm environment plans through the Sustainable Dairy and Water Accord and Dairy Tomorrow initiatives.

Today, more than ninety-eight percent of significant waterways on dairy farms are fenced, so that's over twenty-four thousand kilometers, and over eighty-four percent of farms operate under industry-led farm environment plans. Recent research estimates that those dairy farms who have implemented industry-driven good farming practices have seen nitrogen losses drop by twenty percent and phosphorus losses drop by fourteen percent between 2015 and 2023.

Dairy NZ supports resource management reform and the government's goal of enabling primary sector growth while maintaining or improving environmental outcomes. These goals are compatible. However, as drafted, the bill falls short of delivering on ministerial intent. Our submission provides detailed redrafting that aligns with the structure of the bills. Because many amendments are interconnected, they should be considered as a package of changes. We have been working across the primary sector to ensure alignment. I'll now pass to David to

outline our priority changes. Kia ora koutou. Uh, so primary industries underpin New Zealand's social and economic well-being, as Richard has said, and rely on access to natural resources. The bills must explicitly support sustainable resource use within environmental limits. So we've proposed two key amendments to the goals of the Natural Environment Bill.

First is explicit recognition of primary production as a nationally significant activity. Second is a clear statement enabling resource use, responsible resource use, subject to clauses 11B and 11C of the goals. Our concern is in the absence of this recognition, the Act will be implemented in a way that risks placing too little weight on the benefits of agriculture or resource use as the Act is applied, imposing a hierarchy.

Clause 11B of the goals requires safeguard, safeguarding the life-supporting capacity of air, water, soil and ecosystems, and Clause 11C requires protection of human health. The goals need an explicit recognition of the importance of resource use and the importance of primary production. Without this recognition, if the goals were applied to the extent we are concerned, the implications would be highly detrimental to the future of agriculture and, rural and the national communities.

We also propose additional guiding principles to better bridge the gap between high-level policy intent and the practical implementation. These additional principles act as guardrails to ensure the system delivers the government's intended outcomes. They would build on the existing principles by embedding an enable where possible approach while being subject to meeting environmental goals. Dairy NZ supports setting limits on natural resource use.

For fresh water, it is essential to amend the NEB, to ensure there is a clear distinction between human and ecosystem health outcomes. That is, national human health limits with clear numerical thresholds, regional environmental limits that may also be numerical, but equally allow for qualitative direction of travel approaches where numerical limits are not reliable. Providing for a qualitative approach is essential when setting regional environmental limits.

Catchments are complex natural environments. We don't yet have the tools to translate rigid catchment-level numerical limits into accurate evidence-based requirements at the farm scale. This creates a false sense of precision and risks ineffective or disproportionate regulation. A more flexible qualitative framework would better support and deliver practical ecosystem health outcomes, outcomes. The level at which numerical limits are set can fundamentally determine where-- whether farming can continue as a catchmentAs an example, the Maungawhenuku catchment in Tukituki

in Hawke's Bay, modeling of nitrate shows that to get above what has been regarded as the national bottom line, that is the bottom of C band, you would need to convert all dairy land around three th-- three thousand hectares plus approximately twenty thousand hectares of sheep and beef to forestry. This would reduce sheep and beef from seventy-nine percent of the catchment to around twenty percent of the catchment.

If you wanted to be more ambitious and aim for the next band, B band, you would need to remove all pastoral agriculture, and even then the catchment would still not reach B band minimum bottom lines. These results illustrate that pursuing B band outcomes in these catchments would require land use change. The required reduction in diffuse nutrient losses would be far beyond what is feasible through on-farm practice improvements or mitigation.

This is on an isolated case highlighting the need for careful proportionate limit setting to avoid disproportionate land use impacts. When setting limits, understanding these potential costs is critical, but the bill does not require that. We think that leaves existing gearing and other foreign production highly vuller-- vulnerable. So we recommend that legislation, A, allows for qualitative limits when numerical limits are inappropriate, B, requires consideration of current water quality and land use patterns, and C, requires

transparent justification reports with cost benefit and risk asse-assessments to both inform commu-- regulators and also enable for that, informed community engagement on trade-offs and what we're trying to achieve. So these changes will guide workable secondary legislation and avoid repeating the unachievable limits.

Fantastic. So farm plans are already being used to make decisions in the dairy sector. On the farms we are responsible for, the plan helps us identify where the key risks are and guides our actions and investment to the areas that will make the biggest difference in terms of reducing environmental footprint. That's true across the sector, with farmers using farm plans as a tool for consistent improvement in ways that fit their land and systems.

The NEB provides an opportunity to build on farm plans as an effective tool. Dairy farmers wanna see freshwater farm plans triggering permitted activity status where those activities are best managed through a freshwater farm plan. As drafted, it is not clear they replace existing rules. In summary, we are seeking amendments to ensure certified and auditable freshwater farm plans are the primary tool for managing on-farm environmental risk. Limit the ability to create duplicate rules and regulations.

If the legislation recognizes farm plans as a core mechanism, we get a system that is practical, targeted and effective. This, this can embed continuous improvement rather than layering on added impractical and unnecessary cost. I'll pass to David now to discuss permitted activities.

And we support the government's aim of reducing unnecessary consents and increasing permitted activities. As drafted, the bills, we do not believe achieve this. Permitted activities are an efficient way to manage low impact anticipated farm activities supported by freshwater farm plans, national standards, and targeted regulations or actions where needed.

If these tools can ensure activities do not have a significant impact on the ability to meet limits, and in fact, they can ensure progress is made towards limits that are already exceeded, they should be permitted. Yet under the current drafting, even routine activities such as grazing or track maintenance would require consent in catchments that are considered to be in breach of limits. So that is highly inefficient and co-contrary to the government's intent. To amend this, we're seeking removal of mandatory registration permitted activities.

Although, registration should be available, but not as a default. Clear permitted activity pathways, including catchments at or near limits, provided the package of regulations will result in improvement over time. Permitted activities ex-- supported by what is a, a bit of an intervention framework in clauses sixty to sixty-seven.

Again, we believe this drafting requires refinement. Action plans appear intended to draw on existing catchment and community-led work. Yet as drafted, they primarily function as regulatory tools. So we're seeking amendments to ensure action plans remain collaborative, non-regulatory mechanisms that communities can meaningfully shape and own.

Farmers want the efforts to be recognized before being, before more stringent interventions imposed, but they do not want, want to become de facto regulators, through action plans. Those clauses also in-introduce, what we're calling an intervention sequencing, where the framework also requires... The framework requires decision-makers to consider lower costs, enabling regulatory and non-regulatory options before escalating to more stringent measures. The sequencing is essential to ensure that the system u-uses the least cost, most

eff-effectively tools first, as the first levers before regulators start pulling those more stringent levers. Uh, back to you, Richard. Right. So in closing, the bill requires a package of integrated amendments to deliver a durable system that provides regulatory certainty for primary production while achieving better economic, social and environmental outcomes for all New Zealanders.

These integrated changes will deliver the government's intent for a system that enables sustainable primary production while achieving positive environmental outcomes. Enduring legislation is essential. There needs to be a provision for use of natural resources in a sustainable manner. Farmers have faced repeated shifts in national direction, triggering costly and contentious processes that have consumed tens, possibly hundreds of millions of dollars.

Resources that would have been far better invested in practical environmental action.Dairy NZ is committed to constructive engagement through the select committee process and on secondary legislation. We remain available to workshop drafting and implementation details. Thank you, and we welcome questions.

Look, thanks Richard and David for those insights, and feedback and that submission. Look, I'm gonna give the first question to our select committee dairy farmer, Grant McCollum. Yeah, g'day team. Um, morning. Uh, the just... I, I, I noticed with interest you, in the, uh...

There's a lot of good stuff in here, good submission, thank you. Uh, in the spatial planning area, you, you, you, you expressed some concerns about the fact that, that's not been de- the whole, agricultural land use has, is, is not been dealt with very well. And then also following up with that, you also highlight evaluation reports do not require specific consideration costs and benefits.

Would you care to elaborate on those two points, please? Yeah, sure. Look, I'll pass this one on to David. Thanks, Grant. Yeah. I should say, straight up that spatial plans are really useful. We really support those, strategic plans forward, defining where things can occur. Our concern is that spatial plans will become stringent mechanisms for farming and what we're saying that the limits on farming should be driven through the natural environment of those limits, not directly through or primarily, primarily through spatial plans so that, you know,

where farming can occur and the, the, regulations they're facing should sit within the national environment bill. Yep. Um, in terms of the, cost-benefit analysis, I think it's, that's a theme running right throughout. So, we've got concerns where, f- regulators impose more stringent, limitations, and there's not sufficient cost-benefit analysis. What we're really looking for is something that can inform that, that engagement with communities, tangata whenua and affected communities where, you know, there's that iterative conversation,

"These are the limits we wanna achieve. This is the impact it's gonna have on your economy." "Okay, let's have a look at those limits and, and how we go about it." Th- thanks team. Thank you. Look, we'll now go to Lan Pham. Thank you so much for your submission.

Um, I wanted to pick up on your comments around the action plans. Mm. And you talk about them as a highly regulatory tool, and that you would like them to l- there be, to be more limits on direction-setting and remove the consenting influence of action plans and reduce the regulatory focus. Um, my understanding of action plans, and perhaps I'm misinterpreting this, is that councils actually won't be able to use regulatory tools at all until they're satisfied that voluntary measures, won't actually cut it in terms of the direction that the community's

trying to get to. Can you clarify your understanding of the action plans? I'll pass this one on to David again. Um, thanks, Lan. Yeah, certainly. So as, as drafted, that's, that's the intent there. It seems to be the intent talking to officials. So you go through those, stages, you pull additional levers as, as the issues, arise.

So if you look at the drafting of those clauses, those action plans as currently drafted allow, regulators to start calling in consents. They allow regulators to start doing a, a whole heap of things that we don't consider to be that sort of community-based discussion around what should and shouldn't occur. Now, those levers might be appropriate to pull later down the track, but we just really wanna make sure that those action plan discussions are an informed commun- discussion with community, around what may o- may occur, what may not

occur. Um, you know, is that combination of regulatory and non-regulatory if it's more a result. Um, so basically taking existing catchment plans, building upon that and doing that before we step, further down that regulatory track. So it's a specific drafting and we've proposed, specific redrafting to address what we're, we're talking about or what we're seeking.

Thank you. Uh, now we'll go to Steve Abel. Thank you, Madam Chair, and thank you for the, submission, substantive submission. I, I, I've got a question for you. You're, you're very keen on the only sort of, or the primary means to be farm plan based.

Um, and yet you recognize that we have to look at the whole catchment. No disagreement there with looking at the whole catchment. And then you go on to make a very sound case for why it's very difficult to measure diffusely charged contaminants with any meaningful accuracy at the property scale. Um, and you point out on page 12 that research clearly indicates there are insufficient tools to model diffuse contaminant loss at property scale.

Doesn't that betray the problem that we face that we need to treat, the huge volume of nutrients going into a catchment, and impacting fresh water as a thing that must be restricted at the catchment level? Because it may be that one property in that catchment is responsible for the overwhelming s- main source of nutrient loss. So I, I feel like you can't have it both ways, and I just wonder if you can respond to that.

It's all very well for us to have, non-regulatory consensual buy-in by catchment groups. Fantastic. But at some point, isn't it the duty of the regulator to say, "There is clearly too much nutri- nutrient going into this catchment, and we have to cap the volume going in"? So, so just to clarify, Steve, you're talking about the nutrients effectively entering waterways, or are you talking about going into the catchment, in terms of fertilizer, et cetera, regardless Yeah. Well, I mean, like, for it to get to the waterway, it has to have come in at

the top via urine and fertilizer. Well, so to my mind, it doesn't overly matter how much comes in, it's where it goes. Because plenty of nutrients actually exit the, exit the farm in terms of product, you know, animal product in terms of milk and meat and the likes, right? And then it's... So it's what, what happens with those nutrients as they flow down the line. And the best way to resolve that is through the likes of freshwater farm plans, which basically impact upon the decisions that farmers do make, in terms of how they farm and

what mitigation processes they put in place. You know, wetlands are, are great examples, we've got a few on our farm, in order to limit, nitrogen and phosphorus from actually entering the, the waterway and flowing out of our farm. So- Yeah, that's all, that's all great, but you, you point out in your submission, and I agree with this, that it's very difficult to work out how much is diffusely discha- discharged at the farm level. So, like, that's what I'm pointing out, is the contradiction and the idea that we rely on the farm plan to solve the problem, and

we just have... we don't have the mechanisms, or the means, or the tools to actually know how much nutrient is escaping. It's, it's a... there's a lot of guesswork and ad hocery in that. And that means until we've got those tools, we need to protect the whole catchment with the knowledge that if you put in a whole lot of nutrients, they've gotta go somewhere.

I, I, I... Sorry, if you wouldn't mind, I'll rephrase that as that catchment to scale discussion around actions and outcomes. So there is complexity in these, in these c- catchments, yet there will be some action needed beyond freshwater farm plans, and it might be that further regulations, it might be that you take catchment scale approach, it might be that all farmers, you know, consider where they might put wetlands and some of those critical sort of leaching areas. So yeah, I, I would reframe it as actions.

And I'd, I'd... rather than trying to hit those numerical numbers that may or may not be delivering those outcomes, constant monitoring and iteration of the outcomes. Are there enough fish in the sea, in the water? Um, do, do we have enough shading in this stream here? Do we do some more planting? Uh, so focused on actions, and that's also a behavioral sort of driver as well.

Farmers sitting together talking about what they might do to reduce, nutrient levels to deli- deliver on those outcomes. That really works based on what we've seen. So it's, yeah, more a catchment scale outcomes focus that will translate down to the farm level, but it's about act- actions, that deliver on those outcomes.

Look, thank you very much, Richard and David. We have met time this morning. Uh, we appreciate your, very thorough submission and, and the feedback that you're providing, very valuable information for the committee. So thank you very much. Yeah. Thank you very much for y- your time.
Uh, so we now move on to our next submitter, who we are waiting, Dugald McTavish.

Uh, Dugald actually just needs to accept his promotion, by the committee to a panelist. Um, just need to press that invitation when it pops up on your screen, as we can't bring you through as a submitter until you accept that invitation. There we go. We can see you, Dugald.
Uh, we just, we... Oh. Uh, Dugald, we can see you popping up there. If you just wanna pop your video on and your audio.

There we go. We've got your audio. If you just want to pop on your video so we can see you. Oh, there we go, Dugald. Welcome to the Select Committee this morning. You've got 10 minutes with us, so, in the essence of time, we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission.

Need to gallery. Speaking. Okay, you're small. Can you hear me? Yes, we can hear you. Yeah. Yeah. Sorry, you're on a small screen, but, it's technology, it's a bit... Yeah. All right. We'll get on with it. Thank you very much. Um, so good morning, Madam Chair, members of the Select Committee. Um, first, if you wouldn't mind, I'd like to refer to my submission. So if you could, you could bring it up, that would be good.

Um, okay, I'll fire into it. Earlier in the two thou- in the 2000s, people like Alan Mark, Brian Turner, Graham Sydney, Anton Oliver, and some of us in North Otago were battling to protect rivers from major generation developments in the Clutha and Waitaki catchments. We realized we might win the odd battle, but we're losing the war and would not win it without a shift in our collective attitude to the environment. I see the same flawed mindset behind these bills, so this submission attempts to address it. However, there

has been no clause-by-clause analysis-Instead, I attended two EDS, webinars, noted their concerns, and then asked Copilot three questions. First, what are the key policy and resource implications when we presuppose that virtually any growth is good? This produced table one in my s- submission on page two, i.e. the status quo. Second, what are the implications when we presuppose that overshoot and resource limits are now the reality? This produced table two

in my submission on page three. Third, using the evaluation fa- framework on page four, in a world of overshoot and limits, how well and how reliably would the proposed bills deliver useful outcomes? From the table three findings, key weaknesses are synthesised and an action list for the bills is developed on page six.

My submission is essentially the value of legislation depends on how accurately its assumptions reflect real-world context. And secondly, our current growth-and-development-first economic framing is wrong and must shift towards resilience and sufficiency, including fair resource sharing. These bills are presented as a sincere attempt to manage our environment, when in fact their provisions effectively loosen existing law to streamline development, very similar to the

fast-track legislation. Such development assumes resources, technology, and substitution will continue to support an upward economic trajectory indefinitely. But if we consider the atmosphere's limited capacity to absorb greenhouse gas and the rising risk of irreversible warming, the assumption is clearly false. It is false also because humanity's impact now overshoots Earth's carrying capacity by a factor of one point eight, up from one planet in nineteen eighty. Given this and the

close relationship between GDP and environmental impact, why would we, why would we prioritize accelerating growth? The alternative is to heed the evidence that we are hitting environmental limits and, as a priority, legislate to preserve and restore ecological integrity rather than simply grow.

The Natural Environment Bill includes a goal to enable and use-- to enable the use and development of natural resources within environmental limits, but the emphasis remains on use and development when assumptions about true limits are shaped by ideology.

The action list on page six is intended for a resource-constrained world and synthesizes the, the bills as a whole. Given their length, few submissions-- submitters could base recommendations on such comprehensive analysis, certainly not me.

And while that action list was developed with the assistance of AI, it aligns with my personal experience of how the bills could be improved and would not have been possible without it. So faithfully implementing the action plan in a dedicated part of the NEB would go a long way to quickly align the bills with what science tells us is coming and with the growing geopolitical uncertainties. Some may argue it is too late in the process for such fundamental changes.

But if the underlying assumptions are wrong, then without ground-truthing them, much bad policy will remain uncorrected. In my experience, most of us are uneasy about an endless growth-and-development-first agenda, but keep the concern at arm's length, hoping solutions will emerge without the need for proactive change, despite evidence to the contrary. MPs bring party policy, but surely have a greater responsibility to ensure a safe future for their children. If you agree

we must plan to live within limits, please ensure these bills deliver on that need. Thank you very much for the opportunity to speak to you. Thank you very much, Dugald. Uh, we have a question from Lann Pham. Tēnā koe, Dugald. Good to see you. Um, thanks for your submission. My question is around, picking up on, I guess, the high level of what you're asking for, for in terms of, advocating for future

generations and for very clear limits that actually set, you know, current and future generations actually, not just future generations up for the future. Um, some submitters have raised that they'd like explicit recognition, for example- Of the needs of future generations, and also the need for, climate mitigation and climate emissions reductions to be built into these two bills. Do you have any thoughts for the committee on that proposal?

Um, yeah. Yeah. I, I, I would agree with those proposals. I mean, I think they make good sense. And I think that, focusing on climate is quite a good way of, getting to the deeper problems which, which, which I think is that more general philosophical position, that it's good to grow, almost anything, you know, because it employs people and it, and it keeps the economy stable and those sorts of things, you know.

Um, so but it's, it's almost too difficult to kind of deal with that b- broadly, but if you focus on climate change and we've got to reduce emissions and there's that close relationship between GDP, a- and emissions and our negative impacts, then you automatically deal with a lot of other problems, you know? You, you start...

I mean, if you reduce emissions by 25%, you probably knock back the economy by 50%, and of that order, you know, and that's kind of the thing we gotta do, you know? So, so I think, I think it's a good lever, yeah. Hope that answers it. Thank you.

And now we'll go to Grant McCallum. I noticed one of your recommendations is to add an explicit sufficiency objective and basic provisioning duty. How do you sort of, how do you make that work when, in, in, in light of the fact when people propose solar farms, wind farms, hydro, you know, all renewable ele- electricity for example sources, we get a lot, tend to get a lot of objections to that.

How do you sort of marry those two goals, marry those, marry those things together? So what are the two things we're marrying? Well, the, the objection to building more renewable energy versus- Yeah... when you talk about explicit sufficiency objective for the country. Oh, yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Well, you always have a NIMBY thing, don't you? And,

yeah, I think of the Hayes Wind Farm for instance, you know, that was proposed by Meridian a few years ago. You, you had that conflict between, landscape and, and the reality that we need new, you know, renewables. Um, again, I, I, I, I think, I think the, the the challenge is to sell the overriding importance of, you know, doing the likes of reducing emissions and reducing our demands on resources and so forth.

So I think it's up to the government. I, I kind of feel almost like, I'd like to run a course, well, not just me, but, it would be good if there was a kind of science course or something that all politicians did that kind of helped them to understand that this is such a serious thing, you know, and if we don't get it right, then, and then they can bring the community along, so.

Mm. Yeah. Well, thanks very much. Look, thank you very much, Dugald. Yes, no, we're very aware of how significant, this legislation is, so, we appreciate your submission today, and your feedback on using AI, which I'm sure many of us can relate to as well. So, yeah, appreciate your time.

Yeah. Well, thank you very much. You always get a phone call, don't you, at the wrong minute. Yeah. Um, yeah, so, uh- Thank, thank you... yeah. All right. Thank you very much, and good luck to you all. It's a real challenge, this, so, yeah, all the best. Thank you. Righto. Um, so now we welcome through our next submitter, Linda Conning.
And we can see you there, Linda. Just popped up.

Well, welcome, Linda, to the select committee this morning. Uh, you have 10 minutes with us, to take your submission as read, but we look forward to hearing the main points, and it looks like you have a lot of experience in, in RMA, so we look forward to hearing from you. Thank you. Uh, good morning, Madam Chair, and, mōrena tātou to, to you all, and thank you for inviting me to speak.

I do appreciate that. We do need a system that's not a political football, that protects the environment and meets people's rights and needs, and there's no simple fix to this, but, I'll just give you a short list of some of them. Um, the takings regime should be deleted, the matters of national importance and water conservation orders under the RMA should be retained. Environmental limits need to ma- made clear and enforceable, and that, and, and including the registration of some permitted activities.

Um, I think this would help councils hugely to manage environmental effects. Minimize effects of intensive housing, and provide for urban green spaces, trees, and biodiversity.Third party rights for standing and appeal rights should be reinstated. The system for affected parties need fixing, resource levies retained, and much more.

Now there's a lot of detail of individual provisions in my written submissions, and-- but my submission today is focused on some matters I've got particular experience of and covers both bills. So, I'm sorry you won't really be able to follow me by looking at my written submissions. So I'm st-starting with the goals and purposes of the bills. The pur-purposes include the enjoyment of land and enhancement of the national-- natural environment.

But neither bill actually provides for those purposes of enjoyment and enhancement, nor the goals to be achieved. For example, in the planning bill, well-functioning u-urban areas cannot be achieved if you've got no minimum dwelling size, and with amenity values out of scope, there's no way to provide for those goals. In the NEB, enabling use within limits and no net loss of biodiversity are unifi-- are undermined by the in-ineffective limit setting and the takings regime. This leads me to the specified topics in the takings regime. Selected matters of national

importance from the RMA have been isolated into this category, and protections of them completely undermined by the requirements of the takings regime. Takings provisions arise from property rights as an economic interest of those with access to or power over resources. The right to do what you like on your own land came from the now obsolete notion of the lords of the manor, who nevertheless owed their estates and property rights to the favor of the monarch. Controls on property rights have long been absorbed into

planning, and planning controls restricting development rights protect others or the environment from adverse effect generated by developments. But rights are social constructs, as are planning controls, and all interests need to be considered. Property rights in these bills are one-sided. They may not coincide with the public interest, yet there's no corresponding requirement to provide compensation to those adversely affected by development or the environment. It's no longer acceptable to consider ownership of the commons, such as

outstanding natural landscapes or biodiversity. The property rights lobby is a small but highly vocal part of this community, which has coalesced around ONMLs and SNAs. There is a lot of noise about SNAs suggesting they've been identified by illegitimate means. This is completely false. Ecologists agree on criteria for such an identification, and it's been used since the nineteen seventies and most recently incorporated into the NPS-IB and is standard in most NPSS. RPSs, I s-should say. So a fair process is needed to

encompass rights of all parties, and this cannot occur if both individual and community voices are not heard through public participation, which these bills severely constrain. Notification arises from goals in both bills that relate to externalities and the public interest. But limited-- limiting participation to qualifying residents unfairly discriminates against organizations that might be active in the locality but have no physical address there, and this ignores that there-- nowadays there's electronic communication.

Many people find the planning process difficult and rely on national or regional advocacy groups to advance their views and protect their interests. Actions by our government to shut out participation and the voices of its citizens are disrespecting democracy and should be vehemently resisted. NGOs must be recognized and given standing. And affected parties have rights-- have property rights too, and I think this is important to remember. As part of my master's thesis, I viewed planning applications and interviewed people who were considered affected

parties. Sometimes people were-- who were affected parties were not, treated as such. The poor imbalance-- the power imbalance between residents who were enjoying the use of their land and those seeking to change the land use around them, mostly using professionals to advance those developments, was strongly evident. I found that resource consent conditions that could or would have avoided or mitigated adverse effects on people were not imposed. Basic things such as building a fence, landscaping, noise and activity controls, location of facilities, or

widening of access ways. Even when these mitigations were included in consents, often they were not implemented or otherwise done in a way that didn't meet the needs of the affected party. I found instances where affected parties had given written agreement, but the decision allowed changes such that the person would not have given agreement, or the application that was lodged was different to what the person had given approval for. One example was a large residential subdivision, which was a Christian camp with, various facilities, including

a prayer room, suggested, became accommodation for a hundred and ten seasonal workers working different shifts and throwing liquor bottles over the fence. And there were different sets-- more than one set of plans on the council file. And so what became-- what was a Christian camp became a permanent residential development intensively. Affected parties should be provided with the application when lodged, and then they would know that their written approval still applies. And draft conditions should also be sent to persons

who've given written approval to, to confirm their approval still stands. Otherwise, affected parties' rights are un-unfairly restrained. And these are property rights as well.My research found councils did not protect the interests of rate payers contrary to their expectations, and without legal support, affected parties could not achieve concessions or design improvements from developers. Approving business uses in residential areas or adjacent to lifestyle blocks is particularly problematic. Existing uses frequently expand and

create considerably more adverse effects on affected parties than was initially anticipated. For example, offices with no weekend operations became a car yard, and a bed and breakfast became a function center in a residential area. So reverse sensity- sensitivity provisions in rural areas should not allow existing uses with serious adverse effects to continue indefinitely without any mitigation or minimizing of effects through a review of conditions.

I'll pause there, Madam Chair. Yes. No, thank you very much, Linda, for that submission. Look, we've got a couple of questions. We'll go first to Priyanka. Thank you. Um, thanks for your written and oral submissions, Linda. I just, had a question, about a point that you make in your written submission around the consistency of setting environmental limits. Um, you've mentioned that the fact that ecosystem health limits need to be set by regional councils in their national enviro- environment plan is likely to lead to inconsistency across the

country. Um, and you've, a- and you've mentioned that you would recommend that the national framework, for ecosystem health, health as in the NPSFM should be carried over. To this, you've also talked about, the setting of the methodology that should be done by the minister and, that it should instead be handed over to experts.

I wondered if you could, just expand on that a little bit, particularly around the importance of the national framework, when it comes to consistency versus methodology. Um, yes, sure. Um, I think the only way you can get consistency is to have a national approach as, for example, the NPS, i- all of the NPSs really. Um, and you need to apply expertise in the developing of those national, standards or whatever you want, provisions, whatever you want to call them.

Uh, I think, yeah, if, if, if e- if regional councils are setting ecosystem health standards, you're gonna get different standards right throughout the country, and I, quite honestly, I don't think that, there are such differences in ecosystem, health throughout New Zealand that you would have different standards in different parts of the country. And as you have in this, these bills at the moment, use provisions that allow for, councils to put in different provisions that are not come from the national pr- framework, provided they can h-

provide a justification. So if that, if that part of the, the bills is retained, then there is a process there for, regions to have slightly different standards if they feel they've got different, needs according to their particular environments.

Thank you. Um, one of the, one of the concerns that has been raised by other submitters is the potential for offsetting or the reliance on offsetting when it comes to, achieving the goal of no net loss of indigenous biodiversity. Would you, would you say that having nationally cons- a nationally consistent framework would, would go somewhere to addressing that as well?

Yeah, off- offsetting's very problematic. Um, there are some really good sta- principles for offsetting, and I think that they are in an appendix to the NPS-IB. Um, offsetting doesn't always work. It's a really, complicated thing, and if you're dealing with something that's pr- a threatened species, or a very rare ecosystem type, to... O- offsetting doesn't always provide what you need to get the outcome that you want.

And there aren't very many good examples around the world really of where offsetting has really worked. It, it's, it can be, it can be considered, but it, it can't provide for all situations. Look, thank you very much, Linda. We are gonna have to leave it there.

We've gone, well over time, but we appreciate your time and insights today, so thank you. Thank you. Thanks to all the committee. Thank you. So we're now moving through to our next submitter, Jocelyne Paprill. Welcome, Jocelyne, to the select committee this morning. You have 10 minutes with us, so we will hand it over to you to talk us through our submission. Thank you.

Okay. Tēnā koutou. Ngā mihi kia koutou. Thank you, Madam Chair, for the opportunity to share my concerns with you. I'm submitting as an individual who has a keen interest and involvement in our natural and urban environments. I am active in conservation, volunteering for a number of restoration projects in and around Christchurch. I am also a trustee of two environmental charitable organizations, Christchurch EnviroHub and Blue Cradle Foundation. Over my career as an educator, I have been a passionate advocate of the environmental commons and for good urban planning, which has

community at its heart.I spent a year on a teacher fellowship investigating urban sustainability with a comparative focus on Waitākere and Christchurch. As I stated in my written submission, I have concerns regarding both bills, but acknowledge that there are positive elements within each. I also recognise that the RMA in 1991 needed a thorough review and updating from its original form.

The regular fiddling with parts of the act in terms of amendments means it became a cumbersome piece of legislation that had enabled de-degradation of our natural environment rather than i-its protection. Development that is mindful of nature requires time. It requires all different perspectives to be heard for all possible consequences, including unintended consequences of a development on not only the natural world, but also on human health and wellbeing to be considered. The natural world and humanity are intrinsically entwined, and as nature cannot speak

for itself, we must ensure space and time is given for those of us who are drawn to speak for nature. As we plan our towns, cities and infrastructure, we must be mindful of the needs of the natural world, as well as the future needs of those who come after us. This is the road the RMA 1991 set us on, out on, thinking about sustainability, incorporating long-term thinking and reciprocity into how we live on this land. The RMA recognised interdependence and valued diverse perspectives through public participation resource consent process.

I thought the introduction in 2023 of the Natural and Built Environment Act provided a simplified planning structure with a principle at its heart that I could applaud. Namely, the recognition captured in the concept of Te Oranga O Te Taiao, that the health and wellbeing of the environment underpins our very existence, and indeed must be recognised for its intrinsic value alone. I'm saddened that in the current-- their current form, these bills prioritize economic concerns, with environmental protection as a secondary consideration.

Surely with what we now understand, the reality is that we can't balance environmental outcomes with economic development. It seems under these two bills, we will be returning our country to the pre-1990s Wild West of planning, where adverse effects of development on the natural environment were rarely considered. At least the 2023 Natural and Built Environment Act recognized that economic to-- activity and development are reliant on a healthy environment where biodiversity, resources, and taonga are protected.

Such principles have seemingly been abandoned in favor of unlocking development capacity, delivering infrastructure, and enabling primary sector growth. I therefore cannot support the bills in their current form. I will address in turn the five aspects I'm most concerned about.

One, goals and limits. The bill's goals are weaker than Section six and seven of the RMA, which recognize the importance of things like intrinsic values, environmental enhancement, the protection of areas of significant habitat of indigenous fauna, fauna and kaitiakitanga or stewardship.

The limits reflect minimum acceptable outcomes, the mere capacity to support life. This is detrimental to the need for strong environmental bottom lines. As I wrote in my submission, a development could be effectively allowed without a thorough understanding as to whether the environment can sustain it. A healthy and robust natural environment protects the health and wellbeing of ecosystems in our communities.

Ka ora te whenua, ka ora te tangata. Healthy land, healthy people. As an environmental educator, I've seen firsthand the degradation of our freshwater environments, particularly the lowland rivers of Canterbury. In the last fifteen years, Cove-Fort on the Selwyn River has become less healthy and life-supporting, with more regular algae blooms.

We need goals that provide clear direction for environmental improvement, rather than just achieve no net indig-loss in indigenous biodiversity. Trade-offs such as this could enable the destruction of rich, healthy biodiversity for lesser valued biodiversity in other a- as, as has been canvassed by the previous speaker.

I support the establishment of robust environmental limits at national level, which cannot be weakened by council. Secondly, climate change imperatives. We're experiencing a climate emergency. We see it in climate-related disasters various communities have experienced over the last few years. These bills do not integrate with our twenty-fifty target or emissions reduction plans.

The bills must help our country to mitigate and adapt to the impacts of climate change in a safe and timely manner. These bills have the power to influence where and how we develop land for housing, build more highways, or whether we continue to mine for gold. Yet the bills deliberately exclude these activities' impacts on the climate, which to me is unbelievable.

As a country, we should be looking to the future, problem-solving using natural-based solutions, rather than leaving it to last-minute ad hoc decisions or the policies of insurance companies determine how we respond to sea level rise or the impacts of severe storm. I recommend that the bills be changed to place climate impacts at the center of planning and decision-making. Thirdly, Te Tiriti o Waitangi. The bill's narrow approach to Te Tiriti does not uphold the Crown's obligations under the treaty.

The bills must be changed to meaningfully provide for partnership, active protection, rangatiratanga, and the exercise of kaitiakitanga. Over time, I've-- and I've seen it, Māori have fought hard for their relationship with their ancestral lands, water, wahi tapu, and other taonga to be recognized. Their knowledge and connections have been crucial for protecting significant environmental values. Many iwi and hapū have developed practices, relationships, and expertise under the current Resource Management Act. It appalls me that these new bills seek to wind the clock

back-To reduce Māori involvement to an outdated and tokenistic consultation role. So therefore, I support iwi and hapū having strong decision-making roles within their resource management system as kaitiaki of Aotearoa. I also see fourthly, there's insufficient sup- scope for public participation.

A robust democracy provides for inclusive decision-making processes. These bills do not sufficiently value public participation. It's a fundamental right within our democratic system, people and communities must be enabled to contribute to policy and plans that affect them and the natural environment, no matter whether they live in the area of concern or not. I value the work of watchdogs and NGOs who operate at a national level, but are concerned about environmental outcomes that affect everyone. I come back to the idea of the commons.

We can't leave the protection of our commons to a government minister or couple of ministers. I also fear that these bills seek to weaken the vital supervisory role of the Environment Court. I therefore recommend the opportunities for people to participate in local plans be enhanced, and hearings and appeals to Environment Court are essential.

I also recommend that independent boards of inquiry should prepare national policies and standards. My final one, the concept of regulatory relief, and I see this as the most dangerous aspect of the proposed bills, and it's deeply concerning. I'm opposed to the introduction of the concept of regulatory relief for private landowners, where council seeks to adopt environmental regulations or controls. It prioritises one individual's or an organisation's right to use a resource over the rights of potentially a whole community to access and use this

resource. A glaring example of this is the ability of dairy producers in parts of Canterbury to use and pollute water in ways that blocks the right of whole communities to safe water. It also prioritises the right of existing individual organisations ahead of the rights of future generations to access the ecosystem services provided by a resource.

It may also potentially compromise local government decision-making, as they may be reluctant to make decisions that prioritise the protection of indigenous biodiversity or outstanding natural landscapes, where they may be forced to compensate a landowner. They will prioritise the potential cost to their rating base rather than the long-term cost of environmental degradation and harm.

Nature has intrinsic rights. From my perspective, we're all on this planet together, and a landowner or business should not be compensated for not doing something that may impact poorly on the natural environment. We must prioritise protection of the environment. Nature must have a legal right to thrive. I therefore recommend that Part 4 of Schedule 3 of Clause 92 of the Planning Board Bill, Clause 111 of the Natural Environment Bill be removed. That Clause 122 of the Natural Environment Bill needs to be changed so that relief cannot be provided

for any land use restriction that control impacts on common good resources. The threshold of severe impairment needs to be changed to one where land is rendered incapable of reasonable use. A general polluter pay principle should be introduced to the Natural Environment Bill to recognise the externalities associated- Look, Jocelyn, we're just, we've gone over time there, so we are gonna have to leave it there because we do have other submitters waiting, and we do have an agenda to stick to.

But look, we really appreciate your submission. We appreciate your, passion for this and obviously all your conservation efforts as well. So thank you very much, Jocelyn. Yeah. A pleasure. Thank you. So we now move on to our next submitter today, Suzanne Hills.

Yes. Good morning, everyone. Can you hear me okay? Yes, we can, Suzanne. Welcome to the select committee. We just can't see you at this point, if you just wanna pop your video on there. Yes, there I am. Thank you. Oh, there we go. We can see you, Suzanne. Welcome to the select committee.

You've got 10 minutes with us, so take your submission as read, and we'll hand it over to you for-... your opening remarks. Kia ora. Thank you. I live, on the West Coast amongst abundant nature compared to many other places in Aotearoa, in Te Waipounamu, and this is what motivates me to speak to you today, and I thank you for the opportunity.

So overall, my comment is after reading the bills, attending seminars on them, and reading the submissions of EDS and Forest & Bird, I'm left with the impression that much of the Natural Environment Bill is a facade. So the explanatory note has been written to appear as legislation that lo- that will look after the environment.

But read past that explanatory note, and it's very leaky, and it risks not meeting its stated purpose of a framework for the use, protection, and enhancement of the natural environment. So take the phrase "enhancement of the natural environment". Clearly, that's where we need to be to have a chance of a sustainable future, and it's encouraging to see the wording filters down into the purpose of regional plans and responsibilities of regional councils.

But then it butts up against several walls. So the first wall is that enhancement is not a requirement in setting environmental limits. In an age, in an age of significant and accelerating environmental degradation, environmental limits should be set at a level that protects and heals, not a limit that potentially takes ecosystem health to the wire or beyond.Um, and also one of the Natural Environment Bill goals states no net loss of indigenous biodiversity, and that does not reconcile with enhancement. So it should be net gain.

And no net loss won't protect specific ecosystems. An example here on the West Coast is the unique coal measure habitats of the Denniston Plateau, which is under im-imminent threat from coal mining destruction. So simplistic no net loss could be achieved through financial compensation to DOC for investment in another place like the Heathie, which has happened.

So Denniston and its unique ecosystem with its specialist plants and animals is lost, while the Heathie thrives. No net loss, but we've still lost. And it's not at all clear that greenhouse gas emissions can be considered under the National Environment Bill.

Yet if it's not aligned with the Zero Carbon Act by including emissions, emission reduction plans and emission budgets into the framework, and that's, including it from national policies down to permits, then we won't be protecting and enhancing the natural environment, and we are heading for social, political and economic turmoil. At a recent resource consent hearing, the community group I'm part of had to push very hard to get commissioners to even consider greenhouse gas emissions, which are now permitted under the RMA.

And if it wasn't for our group, we wouldn't have seen an emissions report be required from the applicant, which then resulted in the commissioners agreeing with us and including a consent condition to eliminate the largest source of emissions. Which, by the way, may all go by the wayside because the development is one of the West Coast four listed fast track projects. So it is absolutely essential that greenhouse gas emissions are part of the mix in the National Environment Bill and can be considered as an environmental

effect. I have three further points to make which relate to the small population of the West Coast, at less than thirty-five thousand people, and we live in a large geographical area, the majority of which is either public conservation land or native vegetation on private land. So the first point is the transition time is way too short. So the past few years has seen the development of a combined regional plan on the West Coast, known as the TTPP, the Te Tai Patini Plan, which to date has cost ten point three million and is presently under

appeal from about forty-five appellants covering about two-thirds of the plan. So by the time the TTPP emerges out the other side of mediation and possibly the Environment Court, the region will have to notify a new regional plan under this new act because the transition period is so short. So from my reading, regional plans have to be notified within two years of Royal Assent.

And while I appreciate that the TTPP will form the basis of the new regional plan, much of it will still need to be overhauled to make it consistent with the new act. So that's further financial and participation burden on a very small population base. So there should be some scope for a longer transitional period, particularly in this situation where a new combined plan under the RMA is about to go into the expensive mediation process and then is on the cusp of becoming operative.

So hundreds of West Coast people submitted on the TTPP and some of those stuck through the arduous hearing process, and those people will be utterly fed up if the TTPP hardly sees the light of day before it has to be significantly updated. Second point is regulatory relief is unworkable, it's unaffordable, and it's daft. It is particularly unfair for a region with a small population and with so much of the natural environment still intact.

The most likely consequence of regulatory relief will be the ecosystem health limits to be set by the West Coast Regional Council may not be robust and instead be watered down to invo- to avoid claims of financial compensation or rates relief by landowners and to avoid expensive legal action. We are all both collectively and individually responsible for our life-giving natural environment, and that regulatory relief ref- reinforces entitlement and personal gain rather than community common good and social profit. Next point is the Natural Environment

Bill essentially restricts public participation to those living in the region. So again, with a small population and a vast natural environment, who stands up and robustly defends nature? Well, it's certainly not the West Coast councils. Speaking up for nature has largely been Forest and Bird, and they have invested considerable resources into protecting the natural environment through participating in the public processes.

They have prevented some of the most damaging developments, including an opencast coal mine in Westport's water reserve on the mountain of Takuha. So NGOs bring a nature perspective to the decision-making table backed up by expert evidence and planning and legal expertise. They represent hundreds of thousands of New Zealanders, and to exclude NGOs is deliberate dismantling of our democracy. Cut out Forest and Bird's input on the West Coast and it will be left to a handful of individuals who have the time, the patience and the tenacity to speak out

publicly. And no matter how passionate they may be, they cannot match the expertise and resources of NGOs. So to finish, the natural environment and the climate is under so much accumulated pressure that we have now reached the point where we have our last chance to build a sustainable future. And the bills as they stand could well be one of the nails in the coffin for sustainability.

So it is in the hands of all MPs to work together to get this legislation right, to actually achieve the stated purpose of a framework for the use, protection and enhancement of the natural environment. Kia ora. Kia ora, Suzanne. Uh, look, we've got time for just one quick question, and we'll take it from Lann Farm.

Sure. Kia ora Kia ora koe, Susan. Kia ora. Thank you so much for your submission today. I just wanted to ask, you know, you're painting, I guess, a very clear picture about the implications if these bills are to go ahead unchanged, especially for the environment.

Yeah. Well, fa- I mean, fast track is completely outside of the system, right? This, environmental protection system. So we're putting all this work and effort into this, system of protecting the environment, through these new bills, which are highly problematic in themselves, and we still have the FastTrack Act which, operates completely

outside of that. I mean, we have, y- you know, with the fast track, for example, we have projects on public conservation land where DOC is no longer the decision maker and, and, and we have a purpose in the FastTrack Act, which is, is, is completely different, to, to what the Conservation Act is trying to achieve.

So poor DOC, you know, they're no lo- no longer the decision maker for public conservation land and, and uphold the principles of the Conservation Act. So the f- the FastTrack Act is, it is just a, a, a monster that's been released. Look, thank you very much, Suzanne. We will have to leave it there.

But we, appreciate your insights all the way from the West Coast, a very beautiful part of the country. So thank you. Yeah, for sure. Kia ora. Thank you. So we now move to our next submitter, who was just with us and is still with us. There we go. Diane, welcome to the select committee.

You've got 10 minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to you. Hi. Hi, can you hear me? Yes, we can. Oh, good. Thanks. Um, yes, the RMA needed reform, but sometimes I think, reversing to past systems that worked, like the local catchment boards, is the way forward and could be less expensive and more effective.

Local community agreement and involvement means less need for compliance officers, and compliance can become a self-perpetuating industry with armies of iPad carrying foot soldiers, people with degrees but no real world experience. An example of this at the local level was a situation in Christchurch, where a traffic planner engineer decided to change a corner for safety reasons. Brought in consultants, paid lots of money for the design that no one liked, which had to be changed, costing more money.

And although a few elected members visited the site, the traffic planner engineer or the consultants never ever visited the site to experience what the issues actually were. The end result is a lot of money was wasted on a corner that had non-iner- injury or low injury accidents, and the new design slows traffic on the main roads, which then compels drivers to seek alternate routes on narrow residential streets. So remote central planning never works, but I'm not against some central standards. But, anyway, there has to be the strong local input

there. So farmers are often maligned, but I think they take better care of pests on their land than DOC takes care of pests on public land. An example of cooperation of farmers and community is the Easter Bunny competition. And North Canterbury, they've included feral cats into the competition. Um, so it's great community involvement. There's excellent work also being done in Taranaki, which I just visited recently, where, centered around Lake Rotokare, they are trapping, there's intensive trapping of small predators, rats, et cetera, with

residents, both farmers and townies participating in setting, setting and monitoring traps, mostly on farmland. So, also one of the biggest pests, in my opinion, are pine trees. Eradicating pine trees is a never ending job, and the larger poison trees are left standing, waiting for a spark to start a massive fire.

In Canada, the pine beetle killed off large areas of pine trees, and the fire service begged the government to fell the dead trees, which they didn't do because of cost. These trees then became the fuel for the massive fires a few years back, but which was more costly, removing the trees or the fire?

So something that I feel that needs addressing that I didn't put in my written, submission was, the very real but unseen world of electromagnetic frequencies and radio frequencies. Migratory birds and bees use the, use the earth's natural magnetic frequencies to find their way, and are having difficulty doing this with the ever-increasing wireless use of technology jamming their senses. With the rollout of newer wireless 5G tech without consultation, and no long-term studies being conducted on safety to humans, only industry

telling us that we want it and that it's safe-Yeah, right, I'm gonna trust industry on this one. We protect ourselves from X-rays, and yet use earbuds, bringing these frequencies in close proximity to our brain. Or do the frequencies go through the brain from one bud to the other? Who knows?

How does this f- affect our mental health, our cancer rates? Some want to ban under 16s from social media, recognizing the harm from addiction to devices. Teenagers will soon find a way around a ban, but do you want to increase the harm caused by unknown radiation risks? Do we want to encourage device use through faster dopamine hits? Do we really need our downloads to be that tiny bit faster?

The international guidelines from the ICNIRP are just that, guidelines. Who set the guidelines? Was it industry involved? Did money change hands? New Zealand has turned them into standards. Other countries that use these guidelines also have exclusion zones. For example, in the UK, allowable cell tower and small cell distance from inhabited buildings is no closer than five meters, with 305 meters recommended. In New Zealand, people have cell towers right next to their fence and feet from bedrooms.

No consultation. Industry gets what industry wants. In this case, it was Spark. I'm not being anti-technology, but for safer technology. The position of international groups like Physicians for Safe Technology needs to be taken into consideration. I also recommend that you read all the safety warnings in very fine print next time you buy a new phone.

This is a massive subject that needs truly independent scientific review, not from scientists part of the industry. And the last point is, I want to clarify my s- position in my written, submission about Ngāi Tahu iwi multi-billion dollar corporation, and separate it from the hapū and individual Ngāi Tahu members, who can disagree with the corporation, as they have done with the Green Park huts decision. Ngāi Tahu Corporation has a vast real estate portfolio with a questionable environmental record, both social and

physical environment, and therefore, there is a massive conflict of interest in consulting with them on property issues. So, I mean, I have examples of these if you want them, and I just wanted to leave room for questions. Yeah, look, thank you very much, Diane. Um, that was a very passionate submission. Um, I was just wondering whether you could just elaborate a little bit further on that, as to how you feel the, the bills in their current state, would,

affect that, because that's something you obviously talked about, public, private land, and DOC's involvement as well. And I did hear some submissions earlier in the, I think it was a week ago, there was a lot of hunters talking, and about this point, about not being allowed to hunt on DOC land. But, yeah, so besides the trapping and, the Easter Bunny shoot, and now the, the feral cat shoot as well, um...

O- okay. No, thank you, Diane. And you also made some very passionate points in your submission around freshwater farm plans. Uh, you had some quite strong views on those. Well, yes, we want to have clean water. That's right. And, but the thing is, for some farms, that might not be such an issue.

Maybe they should focus their efforts and their money and their time and everything on, like, control of wallabies, which is a regional thing down in Waimate and, and southern Canterbury, or possums or other pests rather than, I mean, maybe the water isn't an issue on their land, so for all small, or f- what was it, farms over 50 hectares have to do this freshwater farm plan.

So it's tying our farmers up to do something which they should be better focused on other aspects of their environment. Look, thank you very much, Diane, for your time today. We appreciate your submission and coming before us, so thank you.

Well, thank you for giving me the time. Brilliant, thank you. Look, we, now move on to our next submitter, who is a familiar face, and, who has obviously sat on this committee before. Uh, Eugenie Sage, welcome to the select committee this morning. Look, you know the drill. You've got 10 minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to you. Oh, you're on mute there, Eugenie. You just need to put your audio on. Brilliant.

Think I'd know how to do that by now . Thank you, Madam Chair. Tena koutou katoa, nga mihi nui kia koutou. Thank you for the opportunity to speak. But, Madam Chair and members, I am frustrated at having to submit on these bills, because the Natural and Built Environment and Spatial Planning Acts 2023 were much better replacements for the RMA. The integrated purpose of Te Oranga o te Taiao promoted a reciprocal relationship with nature.

It better implemented the treaty. It provided for effective public participation, long-term strategic spatial planning, and a much more efficient and less costly system.And really frustrated because of the very comprehensive and thoughtful policy work, and the considerable agency, parliamentary, and public time and effort which inter- went into that legislation, and which has been largely wasted, in comparison with the haste that this legislation's been developed.

So I suggest that the select committee delay the report back until 2027, and it use the NBEA and SPA as a basis for a major rewrite and to build cross-party agreement on key principles and provisions so that we get that stability and certainty in our resource management system and have law that endures.

Couple of key points. I oppose the two separate regimes for managing air, water, and the coastal marine area and land use development. Nature is interconnected, not compartmentalized. How land is used affects waterways, for example, and there's no evidence to support the siloed approach, and MFE says it's likely to make the system more complex and costly. There is no overarching commitment to implement Te Tiriti or any provisions comparable to Section 8 of the RMA, and the bill's narrow approach only allows consultation, with Maori.

The bills need amending to meaningfully provide for partnership, active protection, rangatiratanga, and the exercise of kaitiakitanga, including recognition of mata- mataranga Maori and the whakapapa relationships with Maori have with te taiao. And Te Oranga o te Taiao, the purpose of the previous legislation, did that. Secondly, oppose the weakening of environmental safeguards by prioritizing economic development. The bills need much stronger purpose clauses to guide implementation.

The purpose and goals should recognize that humans are part of nature, and we have a duty to care for it, just as the environment sustains and supports us. And again, Te Oranga o te Taiao did this. It recognized that the economy is a subset of the environment, not the other way around, and that if we put nature first, we sustain our own wellbeing. We face the twin crises of biodiversity loss and climate instability, which is accelerating. The bills don't address either of these adequately. There's no equivalent posi- provision to

Section 6of the RMA to recognize and protect significant indigenous vegetation and significant habitats for indigenous fauna. Prioritizing SNAs and protecting them is essential to maintain biodiversity. Sorry, just dealing with a call. Without a new overarching statutory goal in the planning bill to protect SNAs, there is nothing to drive natural policy to direct how they should be identified and protected, and it's very difficult to set environmental limits for biodiversity. Under the RMA, identifying SNAs and requiring a resource consent for activities likely to

degrade or destroy them has been a de facto limit. It's been a tool to try and limit, further biodiversity loss. The no net loss in biodiversity in Clause 11 of the Natural Environment Bill is problematic. It normalizes the continued degradation of nature, especially as the bills shift from the RMA approach that adverse effects must be avoided, remedied or mitigated to avoiding, minimized or remedied where practical, or offset or compensated for where appropriate. Terms like minimize are undefined and uncertain, giving scope for degradation and

litigation. No net loss is very difficult to assess. It appears intended to apply at a national level, so that would allow for loss at a district or regional level and compensation to occur elsewhere. And allowing for offsetting and compensation without the detailed guidance which the Natural Built Environment Act provided for the use of these tools, risks normalizing damage to significant natural areas and sites rather than prioritizing genuine, gen- genuine avoidance and remediation. And under the RMA, the functions of

territorial authorities included the maintenance of indigenous biodiversity, and regional councils had a similar respon- responsibility for, in their planning role, for maintaining indigenous biodi- biological diversity. There are no equivalent provisions in these bills which require this of council, so that risks neglect. The new regulatory relief pro- provisions compound the biodiversity loss problem by discouraging plan rules for biodiversity protection, as my submission outlines.
