Look, thank you, Eugenie. I will pass over to Lan Pham, who has the first question. Tēnā koe Eugenie. Thank you so much for your submission and, yeah, the thought and work that's gone into this space over many years. Um, my question was around, something that's come up consistently with submitters, and that is the ministerial direction and the amount of

it. And one thing that has been brought up by others but that you actually mention in your submission as well is this potential to use board of inquiry processes to get out of that ministerial flip-flop that pe- people are worried about. Um, can you expand on why you think that would be valuable?

Thanks for the question. 'Cause they're very broad ministerial powers, there's provision for, I think it's a technical advisory group, but that's a closed process, only advising the minister. Where you've got a board of inquiry which hears and considers public submissions, that's a check and a balance on ministerial power. It makes recommendations to the minister, and it's obvious if the minister deviates from that.It provides transparency, it provides, a better opportunity for public participation, and it reduces the risk

to ministers of being accused of cronyism and influence by corporate and other interests. Because if there's no independent body making recommendations, corporate interests can go direct to the minister, and perhaps that could be, in res- sort of asking for policy change as a result of donations to political parties.

So a board of inquiry protects ministers by providing that independent, recommendatory advice and doing the work of hearing public submissions. And it's been taken out, previously, and I think it does need to be reinstated. Kia ora. Thank you. And now we'll go to Grant McCallum.

Yeah. Hi, Eugenie. Um- Kia ora, Grant... thank you for your submission. You know, I'm just curious 'cause y- you highlight- you focused on the biodiversity, which is great. Mm. And I'm sure you're very aware of the, the, the work done by a farming community in particular- Mm... with QEII Trust and other landowners. I think it's k- I think it's effectively the second-biggest national park in the country if you add it all up. And you s- No, it doesn't have the protections which national parks have Well, yeah, yeah. It effectively creates a lot of new biodiver- protected

biodiversity around the country, which is great. Now, the-- and, and that's done on a voluntary basis, yet you, and you, you, you have real concerns around the regulatory relief where you get something like the SNA situation that blew up in Northland, on the... a, a couple of years ago, and that led to a real pushback from the community and because it was, it was literally taking away people's opportunity to earn as much money, lowering their land values, and that sort of thing. Surely that was a highlight of why we've got to

have, make a, a real focus on where it's appropriate, if a community values that, biodiversity or whatever is significant to them that much that they get compensation for it. Well, I think as my submission noted, the voluntary approach hasn't worked. We've got a biodiversity crisis in Aotearoa, with 4,000 species threatened or at risk of extinction.

We are a biodiversity hotspot because of our level of endemism. I've been involved with SNAs since the RMA started. Mm. Um, and I think there's been a lot of misinformation, and, as there were, was in Northland. They do not prevent people using land. It just ensures that a resource consent is required for activities which degrade or destroy, the values in them.

And there's been huge destruction of biodiversity, but no compensation to the public for the loss of this common heritage. So the regulatory relief provisions impose extra costs on councils. They, there's no guidance as to how they should be done, so that's gonna lead to inconsistency around the country, and it will have, as many submitters have said, a chilling effect on plan provisions which provide, some protection.

Mm. Thank you. So there hasn't been any, compensation to the public for the loss of biodiversity, and yet these provisions are seeking to. Mm. And also Section 87 of the RMA already protects property rights and requires the Environment Court, if there's a unreasonable burden, unfair and unreasonable burden, or it makes land incapable of reasonable use, Section 85 actually, that already protects private property rights.

We don't need this regulatory relief stuff. Mm. Thank you. Thank you. Look, and we'll take one quick last question from Steve Abel. Thank you, Madam Chair. Uh, Eugenie, I wonder if you can expand on the risk of, the, you know, court action with that, right of private property rights to be recognized when it's perceived by, someone who has those rights that they have had them infringed upon unfairly by regional council, and is there a risk that, councils will now get caught up in endless litigation?

Uh, yes. And I think each council's going to have to develop its own framework, that imposes cost, but it is the chilling effect and the fact that we've got a biodiversity crisis that is the, one of the biggest problems. Uh, we need healthy nature to sustain us- Mm... and this regulatory relief will, I think, compromise significantly council's ability to just ensure that people apply for a resource consent if they're going to drain a wetland or clear vegetation which is habitat for significant species.

And there's no protection in the bill for wildlife either. So that's why you need that provision around, SNAs as well. Kia ora. Brilliant. Thank you for the time to make a submission. Kia ora. Thank you very much, Eugenie. We do appreciate your time, and appreciate your very thorough submission as well, so some valuable considerations. Thank you.

Kia ora. So we now move to our next submitter, Andy Mitchell. And we can see you there, Andy. Kia ora, Chair. Welcome to the select committee. We will hand it over to you. You have 10 minutes with us, but do take your submission as read, and then we'll open up for questions. Thank you, Chair. Um, just to know, I, I'm sure there've been some very comprehensive submissions on this very comprehensive legislation, so I've focused in on one part of it, which is the protection of various values and characteristics for the public good.

That's been a key feature of the RMA since it was first adopted in 1991, and clearly it's well-intended, but in reality it's something that has frequently placed councils in conflict, with their communities and with landowners. And as a country, we've made limited progress on many fronts. After 35 years, there are some councils which have protected no significant natural areas, some have protected no heritage buildings, no notable trees, or no sites and areas of significance to Māori. And my local council is guilty of some of that, and I've

explained some of the issues they've had, in my submission. So I think the, first point I'd li- like to make is that, legislative protections have to be well-defined, so councils can implement them, without, contesting with their community what is intended. I've noted there, um-Hutt City Council's, trouble with significant natural areas.

And as a resident, I look through the layers of, regulation and legislation, and, the protection of areas of significant indigenous vegetation was a requirement of the RMA, but there was no clear definition in there of what significance was. There was no clear defin- definition in the Greater Wellington's Regional Council's regional plan of what significance was, or in Hutt City Council's own district plan, of what significance was.

So you ended up with a council arguing with members of its community about what the legislation intended. The national policy statement for indigenous biodiversity was an, from what I've seen, the government's best attempt at this, but it was promptly suspended by the current government. So the national instruments provided for in the bill will be critical. They must provide clear guidelines and assessment criteria for each of the protections required under the Planning Act and the Natural Environment Bill. Uh, my second point is that councils aren't necessarily the best people to do all of this

work. Independent expert agencies are best placed to define and apply protections, not councils. Under the RMA, much of the responsibility for defining, applying, and defending legislative protections has fallen onto councils. Councils don't and can't be expected to hold expertise in all of these areas. Just noting too, other local government reform, redefining core services of local government, and certainly things like, heritage architecture and notable tree assessment are not considered core services of council under that reform.

Uh, the expert advisory group on resource management, reform made a great suggestion, in my opinion, to put the identification of historic heritage, for example, into the hands of Heritage New Zealand as an independent agency with recognized expertise in these matters, and I've noted some other organizations who could do similar on different topics.

Um, handing those protections over to independent expert agencies promotes several important outcomes for protections. One, it achieves accuracy and consistency across councils and across the country. Uh, secondly, reduces the cost to councils as well. So I support that recommendation from the EAG to put those matters into the hands of experts, or at least those same experts should have a role in providing the clear guidelines and assessment criteria, that I've called for above.

Uh, m- my next point is about regulatory relief, and I'm sure you've heard a lot from councils about this. Uh, in my opinion, the whole concept of regulatory relief is a misnomer. It's this legislation that requires protections, so it should be described as legislative relief. And if it's the central government that's requiring these protections, then if there's relief to be paid, it should be paid by the central government. Uh, finally, the regulatory relief framework is unclear in the legislation, which will lead to

inconsistency and contestation. Um, I've talked a bit in there too about how the bills still lack a, a clear definition, this time around significant impact, which is what triggers relief. And I would question, where is the def- definition of significance? Will that be in a, in national instruments? It needs to be defined somewhere, and preferably not through ongoing court action, as is often the case.

Um, I feel that, and the previous spen- speaker mentioned relief frameworks being developed by every council across the country. I think it's another thing that actually should be standard, standardized a- across the country, rather than, than being developed in isolation by each council. Um, the last thing I'd like to discuss brief- briefly is notable tree protection.

It's something I've been involved with locally. Uh, the new bills make no mention whatsoever of the protection of notable trees. Um, I've been informed by, MFE staff that they will fall generically under historic heritage based on scientific qualities. I don't know how anyone would be expected to figure that out for themselves. It's certainly not clear in the legislation.

Um, and I would just support there the submission of the New Zealand Notable Trees Trust, particularly their point that existing schedules of notable and protected trees and district plans be expressly transitioned into the new planning framework. There should be no loss of hard-fought protections through this transition. So I'll just leave it there and open up for questions. Kia ora.

Kia ora, Andy. Thank you very much for that submission. Uh, look, we'll go first to Steve Abel. Thank you, Madam Chair, and thank you, Andy, for your excellent submission. Um, on the, on the notable trees thing, I wonder if you can reflect on the challenge that those scheduled trees under the current regime form a tiny cohort of what- Mm... might be in any urban area, and to not have even that structure of protecting the notable trees, explicitly, defined in this legislation, betrays even the fact there's no talk about having general tree protection or

blanket tree protection- Yeah... which we did use to have up until 2012, which was w- whereby general characteristics of trees that were large, mature, native, important, significant, were protected automatically. Um, I wonder if you can reflect on, what a retreat from any sort of tree protection this current legislation is.

Yeah, well, I think there needs to be something in there to allow for this. Um, I did a lot of work, while our own council was taking nominations of new notable trees, literally door knocking people, talking about the trees on their property, and often they were surprised to learn what was in their backyard and why it might be of significance. Mm. Um, certainly in Lower Hutt, we lost a lot of protection with the removal of blanket protection, I think it was back in 2012.

Yeah. Uh, it used to be that all of the large remnant nikau palms on the Hutt Valley floor were protected. Mm. Um, now they have to be individually named in the district plan, and some of them are, and some of them aren't. And I tried to get some more in there, did get some more in there, but failed to get other ones across the line. So, one thing is I think that there just needs to be a mechanism. If it's not this, then what?

And it's the same with, significant natural areas. So I am now a Hutt City Councillor. One of the first things I heard from the public when I came onto our council and people clocked that I might be an environmentalist is how could they protect their properties, their lands now that council had failed to put through SNAs?

Mm. So, you know, there were some people who didn't want SNAs on their property, but there certainly were others who did, and, you know, they just need to have a mechanism. Brilliant. Thank you. Look, thank you very much, Andy, for your submission today. It appears we have no further questions. We appreciate your time.
We appreciate your submission. So thank you very much. Thank you, Chair.
So we will now move through to our next submitter, which is Te Korowai or Ngā Ruahine Trust.

Aitea Korowai has canceled, and we're not able to bring Te Hunga Roia Māori through, so-... members take a break. Okay. No, thank you very much for that, clerk. So we, will resume at 11:40, and hopefully we will have Te Hunga Roia Māori o Aotearoa by then. So, members, we will see you soon.
